items.question:
A nephrologist colleague asks you for an opinion regarding deterioration of renal function in a 42-year-old woman with primary hyperaldosteronism diagnosed 2 months earlier. Her baseline estimated glomerular filtration rate of 98 mL/min per 1.73 m² decreased to 69 mL/min per 1.73 m² after initiating spironolactone, 50 mg twice daily. A repeated measurement 1 month later showed an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 58 mL/min per 1.73 m². Her blood pressure has been well controlled on spironolactone alone, and her other antihypertensive medications have been discontinued. She has a normal potassium concentration without supplementation. She had initially presented with a 6-year history of intermittent hypokalemia and hypertension requiring 3 to 4 antihypertensive medications. Initial studies showed a morning serum aldosterone concentration of 19 ng/dL (527.1 pmol/L) and plasma renin activity of 0.2 ng/mL per h while taking lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide, and amlodipine. Measurements of urinary aldosterone and sodium excretion were 18 µg/24 h (49.9 nmol/d) and 202 mEq/24 h (202 nmol/d), respectively. Abdominal CT showed normal adrenal glands. Bilateral adrenal venous sampling without ACTH stimulation demonstrated the following:
MeasurementRight Adrenal VeinLeft Adrenal VeinInferior Vena Cava
Aldosterone438 ng/dL (12,150 pmol/L)198 ng/dL (5493 pmol/L)21 ng/dL (583 pmol/L)
Cortisol101 μg/dL (2786 nmol/L)62 μg/dL (1711 nmol/L)20 μg/dL (552 nmol/L)
Aldosterone-to cortisol ratio4.33.21.1
Epinephrine9783 pg/mL (53,356 pmol/L)766 pg/mL (4178 pmol/L)<10 pg/mL (<55 pmol/L)
Which of the following should you recommend?
options: Substitution of eplerenone for spironolactone at the same dosage
Another adrenal venous sampling procedure with ACTH stimulation
Magnetic resonance angiography of the renal arteries
items.explanation:
ANSWER: E) No change in current treatment

The use of a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist such as spironolactone or eplerenone improves blood pressure and potassium homeostasis in patients with primary hyperaldosteronism. It is less well appreciated that there is a decline in glomerular filtration rate in the early phase after treatment initiation. This observation has been explained by aldosterone-induced hyperfiltration, which is abolished with either surgery or mineralocorticoid receptor blockade. The observed glomerular hyperfiltration in patients with primary hyperaldosteronism is associated with decreased intrarenal vascular resistance. The decline in glomerular filtration rate after treatment is significantly lower in patients with low pretreatment plasma aldosterone levels. Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists can also decrease serum sodium levels indicating the reversal of aldosterone-induced sodium-retinic effects and hypervolemia. Urinary albumin excretion decreases in patients who are treated with mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists.

Despite this initial decrease in glomerular filtration rate in patients treated for primary aldosteronism, the decline in renal function appears to be reversible and long-term renal function stabilizes. Therefore, no change in the patient's current treatment is indicated (Answer E). Substitution of eplerenone for spironolactone (Answer A) would not be expected to provide any additional benefit. In fact, on a milligram-for-milligram basis, higher dose of eplerenone are usually needed to adequately treat primary aldosteronism.

of eplerenone are usually needed to adequately treat primary. The adrenal venous sampling procedure in this patient provided good sampling as reflected by the elevated cortisol and epinephrine levels. Repeating the procedure with ACTH stimulation (Answer C) would not provide any new information. Although ACTH infusion is generally recommended during adrenal venous sampling, there is no compelling evidence that it greatly improves the diagnostic accuracy of the procedure. The findings here are clearly consistent with bilateral adrenal hyperplasia. The aldosterone-to-cortisol ratio in each adrenal vein is similar and both ratios are considerably greater than that of the inferior vena cava. Since there is less venous admixture in the right adrenal vein (directly off the inferior vena cava), it is not surprising to find higher steroid levels here. The epinephrine levels serve as another useful means to verify the integrity of adrenal venous effluent; however, the absolute measurements have no diagnostic utility. The epinephrine level in the inferior vena cava is usually very low. Consequently, a laparoscopic right adrenalectomy (Answer B) would not be indicated.

Since this woman had no evidence of secondary hyperaldosteronism that would be expected with renal arterial stenosis, magnetic resonance angiography of the renal arteries (Answer D) is not warranted. Moreover, the use of an ACE inhibitor such as lisinopril in a patient with renal artery stenosis would have caused very significant increases in plasma renin activity, as well as probable deterioration in renal function before the introduction of a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist.

Educational Objective:

Identify the early and transient decrease in renal function that accompanies the treatment of primary aldosteronism

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Treatment of primary aldosteronism

Reference(s):

Reincke M, Rump LC, Quinkler M, Hahner S, et al; Participants of German Conn's Registry. Risk factors associated with a low glomerular filtration rate in primary aldosteronism. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2009;94(3):869-875.

Fourkiotis V, Vonend O, Diearicn S, et al; Mephisto Study Group. Effectiveness of eplerenone or spironolactone in preserving renal function in primary aldosteronism. Euro J Endocrinol. 2012;168(1):75-81.

Sechi LA, Colussi G, Di Fabio A, Catena C. Cardiovascular and ren
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 1: "Identify the early and transient decrease in renal function that accompanies the treatment of primary aldosteronism"

items.question:
A 52-year-old man is referred to the endocrine clinic for management of hypogonadism. Idiopathic hypogonadism was diagnosed 4 years ago when he had presented with fatigue, sleepiness, and reduced libido. He recalls having “low” testosterone levels at that time. He was started on transdermal testosterone gel, 7.5 g daily, which has improved his symptoms.

At the time of his diagnosis, the patient was morbidly obese with a BMI of 42.0 kg/m². He also had difficult-to-control hypertension and impaired fasting glucose. Approximately 18 months ago, he underwent gastric bypass surgery that resulted in clinically significant weight loss. His fasting glucose is now normal and he takes only 1 antihypertensive medication. He continues to apply testosterone gel at the same dosage. The patient has no history of pituitary or testicular disease. He is married with 2 children and does not desire to extend his family. He would like to know if his weight loss has any impact on the management of his hypogonadism.
On physical examination, his pulse rate is 78 beats/min and blood pressure is 128/72 mm Hg. Weight is 219 lb (99.5 kg), and height is 69 in (175.3 cm) (BMI = 32.3 kg/m²). There is no gynecomastia. Findings on cardiac examination are normal. He has white stretch marks on his abdomen. Findings on genital examination are normal with both testes measuring 20 mL.

Laboratory test results (ordered by his primary care physician):
Total testosterone = 591 ng/dL (20.5 nmol/L) (on testosterone gel) Prostate-specific antigen = 1.4 ng/mL (1.4  μg/L)

Which of the following is the best course of action in the care of this patient?
options: Decrease the dosage of testosterone gel to 5 g daily and check testosterone levels in 3 months
Discontinue testosterone therapy and check testosterone levels in 3 months
Replace testosterone with tamoxifen, 10 mg twice daily
Replace testosterone with intramuscular hCG, 1500 units 3 times a week
items.explanation:
2 ANSWER: C) Discontinue testosterone therapy and check testosterone levels in 3 months and case series show that serum testosterone levels are lower in obese men. Data from epidemiological studies testicular disease) than in nonobese control patients. Although the exact mechanism behind this “functional” androgen deficiency remains unclear, the posited mechanisms depend on the degree of obesity. For example, men with mild-to-moderate obesity (BMI <40 kg/m²) have low total testosterone levels (due to low serum sex hormone-binding globulin levels); however, their free testosterone levels are normal. To the contrary, in men who are morbidly obese, even free testosterone levels are low (presumably due to elevated circulating estradiol levels, although frankly elevated estradiol levels are rarely seen). Weight loss after gastric bypass surgery is associated with a significant increase in both total and free testosterone levels (Figure 1). Similarly, weight loss resulting from calorie restriction is also associated with increased total and free testosterone levels (Figure 2). Serum sex hormone-binding globulin levels also increase (due to a decrease in insulin resistance and resumption in its hepatic synthesis). This patient has experienced clinically significant weight loss, and his gonadal axis will most likely recover once exogenous testosterone therapy is discontinued. Hence, discontinuing testosterone therapy is the best next step in his management rather than subjecting him to lifelong testosterone therapy (thus, Answer C is correct and Answer B is incorrect). Effects of Gastric Bypass Surgery on Serum Testosterone
Figure 1. Data derived from Hammond A, Gibson M, Hunt SC, et al. Effect of Roux-en-Y gastric bypass surgery on the sex steroids and quality of life in obese men. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2009;94:1329-1332.
Effects of Weight Loss on Serum Testosterone and Sex Hormone-Binding Globulin
Figure 2. Data derived from Niksanen L, Laaksonen DE, Punnonen K, Mustajoki P, Kaukua J, Rissanen A. Changes in sex hormone-bingin globulin and testosterone during weight loss and weight maintenance in abdominally obese men with metabolic syndrome. Diabetes Obes Metab. 2004;6(3):208-215.
Educational Objective: Assess the effects of weight loss on the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. UpToDate Topic Review(s): Testosterone treatment of male hypogonadism Reference(s): Glass AR, Swerdloff RS, Bray GA, Dahms WT, Atkinson RL. Low serum testosterone and sex-hormone-binding globulin in massively obese men. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 1977;45(6):1211-1219. Hammoud A, Gibson M, Hunt SC, et al. Effect of Roux-en-Y gastric bypass surgery on the sex steroids and quality of life in obese men. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2009;94(4):1329-1332. Niskanen L, Laaksonen DE, Punnonen K, Mustajoki P, Kaukua J, Rissanen A. Changes in sex hormone-binding globulin and testosterone during weight loss, weight maintenance in abdominally obese men with the metabolic syndrome. Diabetes Obes Metab. 2004;6(3):208-215. Pellitero S, Olaizola I, Alastrue A, et al. Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism in morbidly obese males is reversed after bariatric surgery. Obes Surg. 2012;22(12):1835-1840

items.question:
A 35-year-old man with a history of end-stage renal disease secondary to hemolytic uremic syndrome is referred to you for evaluation of hypercalcemia. He required hemodialysis by the age of 27 years, and by age 31 years, he had undergone a successful renal transplant from a living, unrelated donor. Despite treatment with appropriate phosphorus binders and vitamin D analogues before transplant, his PTH concentrations were generally greater than 500 pg/mL (>500 ng/L). In recent months, laboratory studies have documented progressive hypercalcemia and a persistently elevated PTH level (most recent results displayed below). Although not typically recommended in transplant recipients who develop hypercalcemia, the nephrologist caring for him ordered a parathyroid scan (see image). All 4 parathyroid glands were identified (arrows), with greater uptake in both superior glands.
Laboratory test results: Serum calcium = 11.8 mg/dL (2.95 mmol/L) Serum phosphorus = 2.0 mg/dL (0.65 mmol/L) Alkaline phosphatase = 179 U/L (2.99 μkat/L) Serum creatinine = 1.1 mg/dL (97.2 μmol/L) Serum albumin = 4.0 g/dL (40 g/L) PTH = 242 pg/mL (242 ng/L) 25-Hydroxyvitamin D = 33 ng/mL (82.4 nmol/L) 1,25-Dihydroxyvitamin D = 45 pg/mL (117 pmol/L) Urinary calcium = 388 mg/24 h (9.7 mmol/d) (simultaneous creatinine = 1.01 g/24 h [8.93 mmol/d]) On the basis of this patient's overall status, which of the following is the best next management step?
options: Continued observation with measurement of calcium and PTH levels
Parathyroid ultrasonography with referral for parathyroid surgery if size estimates are above 500 mg
Renal graft ultrasonography with referral for parathyroid surgery if nephrocalcinosis is detected
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 6: "See ESAP 2015 discussion."

items.title: Why answer A is correct
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Answer 6: "See ESAP 2015 discussion."

items.question:
A 60-year-old woman seeks an evaluation for low bone density. She has a history of rheumatoid arthritis and has been treated with prednisone, 10 mg daily, for the last 6 months. She has no personal history of fractures, has no family history of fractures or osteoporosis, and has not experienced height loss. She takes calcium supplements, 1200 mg daily (in divided doses), and vitamin D, 2000 IU daily. She walks 30 minutes a day, engages in resistance training 3 times per week, and remains active.
On physical examination, weight is 131 lb (59.5 kg) and height is 65 in (165.1 cm) (BMI = 21.8 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 110/72 mm Hg, and heart rate is 72 beats/min. Her examination findings are normal.

Results from a complete blood cell count and comprehensive metabolic panel are within the reference range. Her vitamin D concentration is 32 ng/mL (79.9 nmol/L).

DXA scan reveals osteopenia with a femoral neck T score of -2.2 and a lumbar spine T score of -2.3.

Which of the following is the best next treatment step?
options: Increase daily calcium supplementation to 2400 mg
Increase daily vitamin D supplementation to 4000 IU
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 11: "See ESAP 2015 discussion."

items.title: Why answer A is correct
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Answer 11: "See ESAP 2015 discussion."

items.question:
A 33-year-old man comes to the endocrine clinic for evaluation of infertility. Three years ago, a 2.8-cm nonsecreting pituitary macroadenoma was diagnosed after he presented with symptoms of mass effects. He underwent hypophysectomy without complications. Postoperative endocrine evaluation confirmed central hypogonadism. He was prescribed transdermal testosterone patches for the maintenance of secondary sexual characteristics and on this therapy he has had normal sexual function and quality of life.

The patient and his wife started trying for a second child 10 months ago without any success. Two semen analyses had shown severe oligospermia (<5 million/mL). He has been referred for further evaluation. The patient does not have any other notable medical history. He underwent normal puberty. He has noticed a slight decrease in testicular size since the hypophysectomy.
On physical examination, his pulse rate is 88 beats/min and blood pressure is 138/78 mm Hg. Weight is 202 kg (91.8 kg), and height is 71 in (180.3 cm) (BMI = 28.2 kg/m²). There is no gynecomastia. Testicular volume is 13 mL bilaterally. The rest of his examination findings are normal.

Laboratory test results:
Testosterone = 542 ng/dL (18.8 nmol/L)
LH = 0.8 mIU/mL (0.8 IU/L)
FSH = 1.1 mIU/mL (1.1 IU/L)

Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment option to induce fertility in this patient?
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 18: "See ESAP 2015 discussion."

items.title: Why answer A is correct
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Answer 18: "See ESAP 2015 discussion."

items.question:
A cardiologist has referred a 25-year-old man with a 1-month history of worsening palpitation, development of tremor, heat intolerance, anxiety, and 11-lb (5-kg) weight loss. He was born with complex congenital heart disease for which he underwent multiple operations during childhood and he has an implantable defibrillating device. In the last 2 years, he has developed paroxysmal atrial fibrillation that has been successfully treated with electrical cardioversion. Sinus rhythm has been maintained with oral amiodarone treatment, 200 mg daily, given in association with oral anticoagulation therapy (dosage adjusted following regular INR testing). Biochemical testing before the start of amiodarone treatment documented a serum TSH level of 2.1 mIU/L and a serum free T 4 level of 1.2 ng/dL (15.4 pmol/L). The patient has no personal history of thyroid disease and has not been the recipient of any iodine-containing contrast reagents recently. His family history is negative for thyroid disorders. = 23 kg/m²). On physical examination, weight is 172 lb (78.2 kg) and the Pulse rate is 94 beats/min in a regular rhythm, and blood pressure is 128/74 mm Hg. He has a mild tremor of the outstretched hands. His thyroid gland is not increased in volume, nor is it tender. No discrete nodules are palpable and there is no bruit on auscultation. He has no signs of Graves ophthalmopathy. Laboratory test results: SerumTSH = <0.01 mIU/L Serum free T4 = 5.05 ng/dL (65.0 pmol/L) mathrm{Serum free T3} = 9.6 pg/mL (14.7 pmol/L) Erythrocyte sedimentation rate = 18 mm/h Hemoglobin = 16.2 g/dL (162 g/L) White blood cell count = 8200 /μL (8.2×10^9/L) Platelet count = 340×10^3/μL (340×10^9/L)
The results of a ^123I uptake scan are shown. Which of the following investigations would be the most useful to establish the underlying cause of the toxicosis? thyrotoxicosis? A Measurement of thyroglobulin antibodies, TPO antibodies, and TSH receptor antibodies
options: Measurement of interleukin 6 and C-reactive protein
items.explanation:
28 ANSWER: E) High-resolution and color-flow Doppler ultrasonography The use of amiodarone, an iodine-containing drug (27%)

The use of amiodarone, an iodine-containing drug (37% of its molecular weight), is complicated by the occurrence of thyroid dysfunction in 15% to 20% of cases, either in the form of thyrotoxicosis (amiodarone-

induced thyrotoxicosis [AIT] or hypothyroidism. AIT is more common in iodine-deficient areas and in men, at variance with spontaneous hyperthyroidism, whereas hypothyroidism is more common in iodine-sufficient areas. For a diagnosis of AIT, serum free  T4 and free  T3 concentrations must be increased and serum TSH must be suppressed. Two main forms of AIT exist: type 1 AIT, which is iodine-induced hyperthyroidism, and type 2 AIT, a destructive thyroiditis caused by toxic effects of amiodarone on thyroid cells. However, the 2 mechanisms may occur in the same patient, resulting in mixed or indefinite forms of AIT. Type 1 occurs in patients predisposed to thyroid autoimmunity or in those with preexisting thyroid nodules, whereas type 2 usually develops in patients without clinical, biochemical, and morphologic evidence of thyroid disease. AIT may develop early during amiodarone treatment, but it may also occur several months after drug withdrawal since amiodarone and its metabolites have a long half-life (up to 100 days) and are stored in tissues, most notably fat, from which they are released very slowly. Diagnostic testing to distinguish between the 2 forms is advisable because the treatment differs—type 1 AIT is treated with antithyroid drugs and type 2 AIT is treated with corticosteroids. Combined antithyroid drug and anti-inflammatory treatment is required in those who fail to respond to single modality treatment and in those in whom the type of the disease cannot be unequivocally determined. It is likely that subsequent therapy for the underlying thyroid disease is required in those with type 1 AIT, whereas subsequent hypothyroidism may ensue in those with type 2 AIT. The decision to stop amiodarone in the setting of thyrotoxicosis should be determined on an individual basis in consultation with the cardiologist, based on the presence or absence of effective antiarrhythmic therapy.

This patient most likely has type 2 AIT, the most common form, because he has no apparent predisposing factors to thyroid autoimmunity or any obvious thyroid abnormalities on clinical examination. Moreover, the very low uptake of radioactive iodine on  {}^123I scanning is typically seen in patients with type 2 AIT, whereas low-normal, normal, or even increased uptake may be seen in type 1 AIT, despite the iodine load. High-resolution thyroid ultrasonography will be informative in terms of determining the presence of a diffuse or nodular goiter, even in the absence of clinically detectable thyroid enlargement or nodularity. Additionally, color-flow Doppler sonography (Answer E) is a very important diagnostic tool that usually indicates an absent hypervascular pattern in type 2 AIT in contrast to the increased vascularity and blood flow velocity in type 1 AIT. In general, determination of uptake of radioiodine and color-flow Doppler sonography are considered the most useful diagnostic techniques in the initial assessment.

The search for thyroid-directed autoantibodies is likely to be informative regarding the presence of underlying autoimmunity, and the determination of TSH-receptor antibodies is relevant only in patients with AIT whose underlying and preexisting thyroid disorder is Graves disease. Because the patient's history and the radioiodine uptake scan point towards type 2 AIT, Answer A would be less informative than color-flow Doppler sonography.

The measurement of urinary iodine excretion (Answer B) is of limited value, as this will be high because of the high iodine content of amiodarone. It would not aid in the identification of the underlying cause of thyrotoxicosis in this case.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 28: "Distinguish between type 1 (iodine-induced) and type 2 (thyroiditis) amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis."

items.question:
A 47-year-old man comes to the endocrine clinic for evaluation of androgen deficiency. For the past 2 years, he has been increasingly fatigued and often experiences daytime somnolence, which is affecting his work performance. He also describes fragmented sleep and morning headache. He recently saw an advertisement on television indicating that men with “low-T” have these symptoms. He saw his primary care physician and requested a testosterone measurement. A morning total testosterone level was 269 ng/dL (9.33 nmol/L). His serum cortisol was normal. He has been referred for further evaluation.

The patient is married and has 3 biologic children. He has normal libido, but he does not report intermittent erectile dysfunction, which is successfully managed with a phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor. He has hyperlipidemia treated with simvastatin and hypertension treated with amlodipine and hydrochlorothiazide. Family history is positive for obesity and type 2 diabetes mellitus.
On physical examination, the patient looks tired. Cushingoid features are not present. His pulse rate is 88 beats/min, and blood pressure is 146/84 mm Hg. Weight is 250 lb (113.6 kg), and height is 70 in (177.8 cm) (BMI = 35.9 kg/m²). His visual fields are normal to confrontation. Acanthosis nigricans is present on the nape of the neck. He has pseudogynecomastia. Cardiac auscultation reveals an S₄. His abdomen is obese without striae. His testes are 20 mL bilaterally. Muscle strength is normal.

Laboratory test results:
Total testosterone (8 AM) = 285 ng/dL (9.89 nmol/L)
Albumin = 3.5 g/dL (35 g/L)

(measured with mass spectrometry)
Hematocrit = 43.3% (0.43)
Fasting glucose = 107 mg/dL (5.94 mmol/L)

Which of the following should be the next diagnostic step in this patient's evaluation?
items.explanation:
46 ANSWER: E) Free testosterone measurement 46 Measurement of serum total testosterone by a reliable assay is the best screening test for the diagnosis of male hypogonadism. Documentation of low serum total testosterone levels (measured on at least 2 occasions) accurately identifies androgen deficiency in most patients. However, when alterations in serum sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) concentrations are suspected, further diagnostic steps are required to confirm the diagnosis. Obesity is one such condition in which circulating serum concentrations of SHBG are reduced. Insulin resistance (resulting in hyperinsulinemia) is posited to be the mechanism, as insulin suppresses SHBG production from the liver. Laboratory studies have confirmed that the addition of insulin in the culture medium containing human hepatoma cell line suppresses SHBG production from these cells (Figure). Indeed, low serum total testosterone levels are commonly seen in obese men. Therefore, further diagnostic steps are required to confirm the diagnosis of testosterone deficiency. The next step in these men is the measurement of free testosterone by accurate and reliable methods. These methods include calculation of free testosterone by mass action equation (using SHBG and total testosterone [measured by a reliable assay]) and direct measurement of free testosterone using the equilibrium dialysis method. The former is more often used because it is readily available through commercial laboratories. Although some laboratories have started performing high-throughput assays using equilibrium dialysis, it is still not widely available to all clinicians.
Insulin Suppression of SHBG Synthesis
Reprinted from Plymate SR, Matej LA, Jones RE, Friedl KE. Inhibition of sex hormone-binding globulin production in the human hepatoma (Hep G2) cell line by insulin and prolactin. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 1988;67:460-464.
This patient is obese with signs of insulin resistance and his SHBG levels are most likely low. Furthermore, his libido (a specific symptom of androgen deficiency) is normal and his fatigue and drowsiness (nonspecific for androgen deficiency) could be indicative of another underlying condition such as obstructive sleep apnea. Therefore, measurement of free testosterone (Answer E) is the next step in this patient's evaluation. This patient's SHBG levels were documented to be low at 0.79 μg/mL (7.0 nmol/L) and his calculated free testosterone was normal at 12 ng/dL (0.42 nmol/L). Polysomnography confirmed sleep apnea and treatment with continuous positive airway pressure improved his symptoms. Measurement of gonadotropins (Answer D) is an important aspect in the evaluation of a patient with suspected hypogonadism because gonadotropins indicate whether hypogonadism is primary or secondary. However, they should only be measured after the diagnosis of androgen deficiency is confirmed, which is not the case in this patient. In other words, their measurement in this patient would have been indicated only if his free testosterone were low. Similarly, pituitary MRI (Answer A) and serum prolactin levels (Answer C) should be pursued only after the diagnosis of androgen deficiency is confirmed. Iron studies (Answer B) are indicated if hemochromatosis is suspected, a condition that can cause either primary or secondary hypogonadism (the latter being more common). The possibility hemochromatosis should only be entertained once the diagnosis of androgen deficiency is confirmed. Educational Objective: Order appropriate diagnostic tests to confirm the diagnosis of androgen deficiency in obese men. UpToDate Topic Review(s): Clinical features and diagnosis of male hypogonadism #
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 46: "Order appropriate diagnostic tests to confirm the diagnosis of androgen deficiency in obese men."

items.title: Why answer E is correct
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Answer 46: "Order appropriate diagnostic tests to confirm the diagnosis of androgen deficiency in obese men."

items.title: How to apply this case clinically
items.text:
## 46 ANSWER: E) Free testosterone measurement 46 Measurement of serum total testosterone by a reliable assay is the best screening test for the diagnosis of male hypogonadism. Documentation of low serum total testosterone levels (measured on at least 2 occasions) accurately identifies androgen deficiency in most patients. However, when alterations in serum sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) concentrations are suspected, further diagnostic steps are required to confirm the diagnosis. Obesity is one such condition in which circulating serum concentrations of SHBG are reduced. Insulin resistance (resulting in hyperinsulinemia) is posited to be the mechanism, as insulin suppresses SHBG production from the liver. Laboratory studies have confirmed that the addition of insulin in the culture medium containing human hepatoma cell line suppresses SHBG production from these cells (Figure). Indeed, low serum total testosterone levels are commonly seen in obese men. Therefore, further diagnostic steps are required to confirm the diagnosis of testosterone deficiency. The next step in these men is the measurement of free testosterone by accurate and reliable methods. These methods include calculation of free testosterone by mass action equation (using SHBG and total testosterone [measured by a reliable assay]) and direct measurement of free testosterone using the equilibrium dialysis method. The former is more often used because it is readily available through commercial laboratories. Although some laboratories have started performing high-throughput assays using equilibrium dialysis, it is still not widely available to all clinicians.
Insulin Suppression of SHBG Synthesis
Reprinted from Plymate SR, Matej LA, Jones RE, Friedl KE. Inhibition of sex hormone-binding globulin production in the human hepatoma (Hep G2) cell line by insulin and prolactin. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 1988;67:460-464.
This patient is obese with signs of insulin resistance and his SHBG levels are most likely low. Furthermore, his libido (a specific symptom of androgen deficiency) is normal and his fatigue and drowsiness (nonspecific for androgen deficiency) could be indicative of another underlying condition such as

items.question:
A 72-year-old man is referred to the endocrine clinic for further management of late-onset hypogonadism. Testosterone deficiency was diagnosed 7 months ago after he presented to his primary care physician with symptoms of impaired concentrating ability, reduced vigor, and weight gain. He did not have sexual dysfunction or loss of body hair. His morning total testosterone concentration was 293 ng/dL (10.2 nmol/L). He was prescribed axillary testosterone solution, 60 mg daily. Although adherent to this medication regimen, he reported lack of benefit to his doctor, which triggered this referral.

The patient continues to have mental cloudiness. He is also unhappy because he has not lost any weight on testosterone therapy. He is requesting another modality of testosterone replacement that might be more effective. He has a decade-long history of hypertension treated with hydrochlorothiazide and gout treated with probenecid. Otherwise he is healthy. He lives with his wife of 40 years.
On physical examination, he is a lively, overweight man. Weight is 193 lb (87.7 kg), and height is 68 in (172.7 cm) (BMI = 29.3 kg/m²). Pulse rate is 76 beats/min, and blood pressure is 138/64 mm Hg. The rest of his examination findings are normal.

Laboratory test results:
Testosterone = 487 ng/dL (16.9 nmol/L)
Prostate-specific antigen = 2.3 ng/mL (2.3  μg/L)
Hematocrit = 42.8% (0.428)

Which of the following is the most appropriate course of action in this patient's care?
options: Change testosterone therapy to transdermal gel
Change testosterone therapy to buccal testosterone
items.explanation:
## 70 ANSWER: D) Discontinue testosterone therapy The last decade has seen a tremendous surge in testosterone prescriptions resulting in a significant increase in testosterone sales. This increase is mainly driven by prescriptions written for middle-aged and older men with late-onset hypogonadism or age-related decline in testosterone levels. Male aging is associated with decreased circulating serum testosterone levels. At the same time, aging is also associated with a plethora of clinical manifestations such as loss of muscle mass, frailty, loss of vigor, memory loss, and body composition changes. Since many of these clinical manifestations are seen in men with pathologic hypogonadism (pituitary or testicular disease), it is often assumed that these age-related manifestations are a consequence of testosterone decline. This is not an evidence-based assumption because it is unclear whether these age-related conditions are independent of testosterone or a result of its decline. Therefore, in older men who do not have pathologic hypogonadism, testosterone therapy should only be initiated on a case-by-case basis. Some symptoms of testosterone deficiency are very specific (such as decreased libido, loss of morning erections, gynecomastia, low trauma fractures, hot flashes), while many are nonspecific (such as poor concentration, loss of muscle, fatigue, body composition changes) (Table). This patient's symptoms were nonspecific to start with, and despite being on testosterone therapy for 7 months with therapeutic serum levels, he has not experienced any benefit. In these cases, physicians should discontinue testosterone treatment (Answer D) rather than subject a patient to lifelong androgen therapy.
Table. Signs and Symptoms of Androgen Deficiency.
More Specific Symptoms | Less Specific Symptoms Incomplete sexual development | Decreased energy and motivation Eunuchoidism | Decreased initiative and confidence Reduced libido (sexual desire) | Feeling sad, depressed mood Decreased spontaneous erections | Poor concentration and memory Gynecomastia | Sleep disturbance, hypersomnolence Loss of body hair (pubis, axilla) | Mild anemia (normocytic, normochromic) Very small testes (<5 mL) | Reduced muscle bulk and strength Infertility (low sperm count) | Increased body fat and BMI Minimal trauma fracture, low bone density | Diminished work performance Hot flashes Adapted from Bhasin S, Cunningham GR, Hayes FJ, et al; Task Force, Endocrine Society. Testosterone therapy in men deficiency syndromes: an Endocrine Society clinical pract
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 70: "Discontinue testosterone therapy in an older man with late-onset hypogonadism when no beneficial effects are seen."

items.question:
A 28-year-old man is referred to your clinic for management of hypogonadism. The patient has a history of β-thalassemia major that has resulted in transfusion-related secondary hemochromatosis. He presented to an outside endocrinologist 5 months ago with concerns of sexual dysfunction, fatigue, and low mood. Detailed evaluation revealed isolated secondary hypogonadism. Findings on pituitary MRI were normal. He was initially prescribed the transdermal testosterone patch, but his insurance would not cover it because of the high monthly cost. As a result, his regimen was switched to intramuscular testosterone cypionate, 200 mg every 2 weeks. On this regimen, in general, the patient has felt better. However, he is very bothered by symptoms of lethargy and low mood that occur a few days before the next injection. This affects his libido and also interferes with his work performance. He mentioned this to his endocrinologist who has referred him to you.
On physical examination, the patient has a bronze tinge to his skin. Pulse rate is 84 beats/min, and blood pressure is 119/78 mm Hg. Weight is 183 lb (83.2 kg), and height is 70 in (177.8 cm) (BMI = 26.3 kg/m²). Cardiac examination reveals a low-grade systolic murmur. The liver edge is palpable. His phallus is normal. His right testis is 12 mL and the left testis is 15 mL.

Laboratory test results (from outside endocrinologist):
Testosterone (assessed midway between testosterone injections) = 845 ng/dL (29.3 nmol/L)
Hematocrit = 36.2% (0.362)

Which of the following is the best next step in this patient's management?
options: Switch to testosterone enanthate at the current dosage
Change the current regimen to weekly injections of 100 mg
Change the current regimen to monthly injections of 400 mg
Discontinue injections and start transdermal gel, 5 g daily
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 79: "See ESAP 2015 discussion."

items.title: Why answer A is correct
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Answer 79: "See ESAP 2015 discussion."

items.question:
A 53-year-old man with chronic back pain is referred by his internist for counseling regarding sexual adverse effects of opioid analgesic use. The patient has a 14-month history of chronic back pain in the lumbar region as a result of motor vehicle crash. Radiologic evaluation revealed only some degenerative changes in the spine. Thus far, his pain has been managed with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents. However because of worsening pain, his internist would like to prescribe an opioid analgesic. While discussing the various adverse effects of opioids, his doctor also mentioned sexual adverse effects. This is concerning for the patient because he already has a history of intermittent erectile dysfunction. He has been referred to the endocrine clinic for further counseling and also to learn which opioid analgesic least influences the gonadal axis.

The patient has no history of pituitary or testicular disease. He is married and has 2 biologic children. He has normal libido, but experiences intermittent erectile dysfunction that is successfully managed with sildenafil. He reports that the frequency of sexual activity has decreased because of worsening back pain.
On physical examination, pulse rate is 78 beats/min and blood pressure is 134/78 mm Hg. Weight is 197 lb (89.5 kg), and height is 71 in (180.3 cm) (BMI = 27.5 kg/m²). His physical examination findings are notable only for tenderness in the lower back.

His total testosterone concentration (drawn in the morning at his primary care physician's office) is 491 ng/dL (17.04 nmol/L).

Given his concerns, which analgesic would be the best option for this patient?
items.explanation: 115 ANSWER: D) Buprenorphine 115 Chronic nonmalignant pain lasting longer than 6 months affects 15% to 30% of the population. Most of these patients benefit from combined treatment with physical modalities (warm compresses, acupuncture) and nonopioid analgesics (mainly nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents). However, approximately 20% to 25% of these patients do not benefit from traditional measures and require treatment with opioid analgesics. The last decade has seen a significant increase in the prescription of opioid analgesics for the treatment of noncancer and cancer-related chronic pain. As a result, physicians are increasingly encountering patients experiencing adverse effects of opioid therapy, including endocrine adverse effects. Although perturbations in adrenal, thyroid, and somatotropic axes have been reported, opioid-induced testosterone deficiency is the most commonly encountered endocrine adverse effect. Studies have shown that men taking opioid analgesics have testosterone levels that are half as high as testosterone levels of their age-matched counterparts taking nonopioid analgesics. This suppressive effect of opioid analgesics on gonadal hormones is seen regardless of their route of administration. Low testosterone levels are observed in 86% of men receiving intrathecal opioids, 89% receiving oral opioids, and 80% receiving transdermal opioids. In some patients, testosterone levels even decline into the castrate range (<50 ng/dL [<1.74 nmol/L]). Affected patients generally present with decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, and fatigue. In addition to sexual dysfunction, these patients also have a high prevalence of osteopenia and osteoporosis. As expected, men with opioid addiction experience similar hormonal perturbations as do patients on prescription opioid analgesics. Opioid analgesics suppress testosterone levels by inhibiting the synthesis and secretion of GnRH; thus, most patients present with a hormonal pattern consistent with secondary hypogonadism (low or inappropriately normal gonadotropins with low serum testosterone levels). Evidence suggests that this suppression of GnRH synthesis is mediated via the μ-opioid receptor. Hence, opioid analgesics that are agonists at the μ-opioid receptor suppress the gonadal axis. These include natural derivatives of opium such as morphine (Answer E), semisynthetic opioid analgesics such as oxycodone (Answer B) and fentanyl (Answer C), and synthetic opioid analgesics such as methadone (Answer A). Hence, all of the above-mentioned medications have the potential to suppress serum testosterone levels in this patient and are therefore not the best choices. Buprenorphine (Answer D) does not suppress the gonadal axis because of its unique pharmacologic profile. It is a partial agonist at the μ-opioid receptor and an antagonist at the kappa-opioid receptor. These differing effects at the p- and x-opioid receptors are posited to be the mechanism behind its sparing of the gonadal axis. Buprenorphine is a potent opioid analgesic that was originally approved in the treatment of opioid dependence; however, it is used in the treatment of both noncancer and cancer-related chronic pain. In a study of men with opioid dependence, serum testosterone levels were normal in patients treated with buprenorphine and were comparable to levels in age-matched control participants (Figure 1). Methadone-treated patients had significantly lower levels of serum testosterone. As a result, both libido and erectile function were normal in most men on buprenorphine compared with abnormal function in those on methadone (Figure 2). The patient in this vignette experienced a substantial improvement in his back pain with buprenorphine, while his testosterone levels remained within the normal range.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 115: "Recommend the opioid analgesic least likely to suppress testosterone levels and review the effects of opioid analgesics on the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis."

items.title: Why answer D is correct

items.question:
A man on testosterone replacement has symptomatic improvement. PSA rose from 1.2 to 2.9 ng/mL over 12 months (increase 1.7 ng/mL). Digital exam shows benign prostatic hyperplasia without urinary symptoms.

Best next step?
items.explanation: Endocrine Society guidelines recommend urologic referral when PSA rises >1.4 ng/mL on testosterone therapy—this triggers evaluation, not automatic cancer diagnosis.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 103: "Monitor serum prostate-specific antigen levels during testosterone therapy."

items.title: Why answer A is correct
items.text: Endocrine Society guidelines recommend urologic referral when PSA rises >1.4 ng/mL on testosterone therapy—this triggers evaluation, not automatic cancer diagnosis.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 103: "Monitor serum prostate-specific antigen levels during testosterone therapy."

items.title: How to apply this case clinically
items.text: Monitor PSA on TRT; a confirmed rise >1.4 ng/mL warrants urology referral even if absolute PSA remains 'normal'.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 103: "Monitor serum prostate-specific antigen levels during testosterone therapy."

items.statement: PSA rise >1.4 ng/mL on testosterone therapy warrants urology referral.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 103: "PSA increase greater than 1.4 ng/mL"

items.statement: Weight loss can reverse functional hypogonadism in obese men.
items.explanation: Bariatric surgery and lifestyle increase testosterone.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 2: "discontinue testosterone therapy and check levels"

items.statement: Buprenorphine may have less HPA suppression than traditional opioids.
items.explanation: Partial agonist may spare gonadal axis more.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 115: "buprenorphine"
ESAP 2015 Case 18: "Fertility treatment"

items.statement: Free testosterone measurement is useful when SHBG is abnormal.
items.explanation: Obesity and liver disease alter SHBG.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 46: "Free testosterone measurement"

items.statement: Normal morning total testosterone excludes organic hypogonadism in all ED patients.
items.explanation: ED multifactorial; isolated low-normal T may still matter.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 115: "Erectile dysfunction"

items.statement: hCG stimulates testicular testosterone in hypogonadotropic hypogonadism desiring fertility.
items.explanation: Preserves or restores spermatogenesis vs exogenous T.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 18: "hCG injections"

items.statement: 5-alpha reductase inhibitors are mandatory on all TRT.
items.explanation: Only if LUTS/BPH indications; not routine.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 103: "dutasteride is not indicated"

items.assertion: A PSA of 2.9 ng/mL on TRT always indicates prostate cancer.
items.reason: Rise triggers evaluation; does not equal cancer.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 103: "does not indicate the presence of prostate cancer"

items.assertion: Continued TRT is best after major weight loss with normalized testosterone.
items.reason: Exogenous T should stop to assess recovery of axis.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 2: "Discontinue testosterone therapy"

items.assertion: Testosterone is ideal therapy when pursuing pregnancy.
items.reason: Exogenous T inhibits spermatogenesis; use hCG/FSH.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 18: "hCG injections"

items.reason: Potency and opioid type differ; buprenorphine may be less suppressive.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 115: "buprenorphine"

items.reason: PDE5 inhibitors and TRT can coexist when indicated.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 115: "sildenafil"

items.assertion: Reassurance is appropriate when PSA rises 1.7 ng/mL on TRT.
items.reason: Exceeds 1.4 ng/mL threshold; refer to urology.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 103: "referred for urologic evaluation"

items.assertion: Klinefelter syndrome never produces sperm with assisted reproduction.
items.reason: Micro-TESE may retrieve sperm in some.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "Klinefelter"

items.assertion: Total testosterone is always adequate in obesity.
items.reason: Low SHBG can mask low free T; measure free/bioavailable T.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 46: "Free testosterone measurement"
